_README.instructions: Copy this file as {lang}.json and translate the values. Keys (the left-hand side) must NEVER change — they map to data-i18n attributes in index.html.

w02.title: Standby vs. OFF — Hidden Energy Consumption
w02.topic: 01: Efficient Use of Energy — Entertainment & Electronics
w02.obj2: Compare power usage in different device states.
w02.obj3: Calculate annual energy waste and its cost.
w02.resources: TV / console / monitor specifications (or internet), calculator, power meter (optional).
w02.intro1: Many electronic devices continue to consume energy even when they are “turned off” — as long as they remain plugged in. This is called standby power, or more colourfully, vampire energy. A small LED, a clock display, or a wireless receiver waiting for a remote signal all draw a few watts continuously.
w02.intro2: Individually it seems trivial. Across a household — TVs, game consoles, monitors, chargers, microwaves — it adds up to a noticeable share of the electricity bill, and to wasted carbon emissions. In this worksheet you will quantify exactly how much is wasted and what it costs.
w02.formulaIntro: The formula below converts continuous power draw into yearly energy use. For standby, assume the device sits in standby for 20 hours per day.
w02.varH: hours per day in that state

act1.intro: List three devices in your home that go into standby (a small LED stays on when you press “off”).

act2.intro: Look up each device’s power in three states. Use the user manual, the back-label, an online datasheet, or a power meter if you have one.

act3.intro: Take the TV from your table. Use the formula to calculate its yearly standby energy.

act4.intro: Find your local electricity price per kWh and calculate the yearly cost of that TV standby.

act5.intro: Imagine a typical home with 10 devices sitting in standby. Estimate the total annual cost (you may assume an average standby of 5 W per device).

didYouKnow.title: 💡 Did you know?
didYouKnow.body: Turning off devices at the wall socket — instead of leaving them on standby — can save up to 10 % of household electricity. A switched power strip lets you cut several devices at once with a single flick.

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.prompt: Explain in your own words why standby energy is sometimes called “invisible consumption”, and how it affects the energy use of your own home.
